content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

April 29, 1977

Jeffrey N. Haney
Deputy City Attorney
14th and Washington Streets
Oakland, California 94612

Dear Mr. Haney:

Please excuse the delay in responding to your letter of January 14, 1977, requesting an opinion as to whether Mr. McDonnell's position as Executive Director of the Association of Oakland Employees would require him to abstain from participating in decisions of the Board of Administration of the Oakland Municipal Employees Retirement System.

Government Code Section 87100 1/ provides that:

"No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest."

As a member of the OMERS Board of Administration, Mr. McDonnell clearly is a public official within the meaning of Sections 82048 and 82041. Further, in his position as a Board member, he makes and participates in making governmental decisions within the meaning of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18700 (b) and (c). Does he have a financial interest", however, within the meaning of Section 87103 2/?

_________________________________

1/ Unless otherwise indicated, all citations are to the California Government Code.

2/ Section 87103 provides, in pertinent part:

"87103. Financial Interest. An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000);

(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000);

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION (916) 322 5660 • EXECUTIVE LEGAL 322 5591 • ENFORCEMENT CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322 6441

Mr. Jeffrey N. Haney
April 29, 1977
page two

Initially, we observe that the Association of Oakland Employees would
not seem to be a "business entity" within the meaning of Section 82605 as
it would not appear to be an organization or enterprise operated for profit.
As Section 87103(a) and (d) are tied to interests in a "business entity",
they are not applicable to this case. Nor, is Section 87103(b), which
involves real property, applicable. As the Association is a source of
income to Mr. McDonnell, however, Section 87103(c) is relevant. Applying
the standards in Section 87103, we must consider whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that a decision made or participated in by Mr. McDonnell as a
member of the OMERS Board, will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the Association,
Mr. McDonnell's source of income.

As your letter recites, the Board decides whether or not persons are
entitled to benefits under the retirement system; how retirement funds are
to be administered and invested; and, pursuant to city ordinance, the city's
rate of contribution to the system. It also recommends pension benefit
increases to the City Council. While all of these decisions, foreseeably,
may effect individual retirees, current city employees, and/or the city,
it is difficult to see how these decisions will directly effect the Association, Mr. McDonnell's source of income. Arguably, to increase benefits,
findings of non-entitlement, or bad investment decisions will economically
weaken Association members whose pension benefits will suffer and, ultimately, this might weaken member support for the Association but this is a
very tenuous cause and effect relationship at best and it is simply not
possible for us to conclude that it is reasonably foreseeable that Board
decisions will have a material financial effect on the Association, Mr. McDonnell's source of income. Having reached this decision with regard to foreseeability, it is not necessary to consider the issue of materiality. Thus, in response to your question, we conclude that Mr. McDonnell's position as Executive Director of the Association of Oakland Employees would not require him to abstain from participation in decisions of the OMERS Board.

2/ (continued)

(c) Any source of income, other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value received or promised to the public official within twelve months prior to the time when the decision is made; or

(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.

Mr. Jeffrey N. Haney
April 28, 1977
page three

     I hope that this informal advice, which is based on the facts contained
in your letter, will be of some assistance to you. I enclose for your
review a copy of the Moore Opinion which was recently issued by the Commission and which involves a member of the governing board of a local government retirement system. Please let me know if we can be of further assistance
to you.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Edgar A. Kerry
                                        Staff Attorney
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division
                                        916/322-6444

EAK:mfa
Attachment

FPPC
JAN 18 W 48 M'77

CITY OF OAKLAND

CITY HALL • 14TH AND WASHINGTON STREETS • OAKLAND, CALIFORNIA 94612

Office of the City Attorney
David A. Self
City Attorney

January 14, 1977

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Request for Opinion

Gentlemen:

The City Attorney's office of the City of Oakland, on behalf
of James L. McDonnell, a member of the Oakland Municipal
Employees' Retirement System Board of Administration,
respectfully requests the opinion of the Fair Political
Practices Commission concerning the interpretation of
Chapter VIII, Article I of the Political Reform Act of 1974.

The Oakland Municipal Employees' Retirement System (OMERS)
is a retirement system established pursuant to the Charter
of the City of Oakland. Prior to 1970, all employees of the
City, with the exception of police and fire personnel, were
members of this retirement system. In 1970, the City
entered into a contract with the California Public Employees'
Retirement System which permitted OMERS members to transfer
to the State system. As a result of the original transfer
pursuant to the contract in 1970 and two subsequent amendments
to that agreement which permitted additional transfers, the
active membership of employees currently employed by the
City of Oakland in OMERS is approximately 34. Except in
rare instances where former employees who are members of the
OMERS system return to work with the City, no new members
will join the OMERS system. At the present time, there are
approximately 700 retired employees who receive pensions
from the retirement system.

Section 2015 of the Charter of the City of Oakland sets
forth the basic duties of members of the Board of
Administration of OMERS. This section provides that the
Board of Administration shall be the sole judge in
determining whether or not persons are entitled to benefits
of any sort under the retirement system, and they are given
exclusive control of the administration and investment of
the retirement funds. The Board of Administration,

Fair Political Practices Commission
January 14, 1977
Page 2

pursuant to ordinance, also determines the City's rate of
contribution to the retirement system. Although the decision
whether or not to increase the pension benefits under the
system, including those already retired, is vested in the
City Council, the OMERS Board of Administration has made
recommendations to the City Council concerning increases in
pension benefits. Any increase in pension benefits could be
funded from either surplus earnings from the funds of the
retirement system or through additional contributions from
the general funds of the City.

The Oakland Municipal Civil Service Employees' Association
(Association) is an organization of City of Oakland employees
having as its same the furthering of the interest of City
employees and their relationship with their employer. At
the present time, the Association represents a small number
of employees in formal labor negotiations with the City,
although membership in the Association is not limited to
those employees. Only one member of the Association presently
employed by the City is an active OMERS member. We are also
informed that the Association has approximately 130 associate
members who are retired City employees and are presently
receiving OMERS pension. It is the aim of the Civil Service
Employees' Association to maximize the retirement benefits
for these associate members.

Mr. McDonnell receives $400 per month as Executive Director
of the Association. Out of this fund Mr. McDonnell is
required to provide clerical and office services to the
Association. These services are provided to the Association
through Ascott Funding, a corporation which is wholly owned
by Mr. McDonnell and his wife.

Mr. McDonnell desires the Commission's opinion as to whether
his position as Executive Director of the employees' association
would require him to abstain from participating in decisions
of the OMERS Board which affect its responsibilities as
outlined herein.

Very truly yours,

DAVID A. SELF
City Attorney

By: Jeffrey N. Haney
Deputy City Attorney

JNH:am

cc: Kenneth A. Maul
